coredata.dc:title: Glucose-based carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Ning
Chen, Jia
Cai, Tianpei
Li, Zhan
Guan, Ming
Zhao, Liang
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: A new stationary phase based on glucose-derived carbon dots-modified on silica (Sil-Glc-CDs) was prepared and applied in hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). The Sil-Glc-CDs column showed better retention ability and separation selectivity toward polar analytes, including amino acids, saccharides, ginsenosides, antibiotics, nucleosides, and nucleobases when compared with a commercial HILIC column and a home-made glucose-modified silica (Sil-Glc) column. The effect of the organic phase ratio, salt concentration, pH and column temperature on chromatographic retention behavior was investigated. In addition, the column exhibited good stability and column-to-column reproducibility (RSDs 0.80–1.97%, n = 3). Particularly, this column was successfully applied for the determination of the concentration of glucose (2.2 mg mL−1) and fructose (3.4 mg mL−1) in the goji berry solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose carbon dots
Stationary phase
HILIC
Chromatographic separation
Goji berry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Sil-Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc stationary phases
Stability and reproducibility of the Sil-Glc-CDs column
Quantitative detection of fructose and glucose from goji berry solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedure of Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase.
TEM images (a) Bare silica, (b) Glc-CDs, (c) Sil-Glc-CDs, (d) LSCM images of Sil-Glc-CDs.

Effect of (a) acetonitrile concentration, (b) salt concentration, (c) pH values, (d) column temperature on the retention factor (k) of nucleoside and nucleobases in the Sil-Glc-CDs. Analytes: (1) thymine, (2) thymidine, (3) uridine, (4) adenine nucleoside, (5) adenine, (6) cytosine, (7) cytosine nucleoside, (8) guanosine nucleoside, (9) astragalus nucleoside. Chromatographic conditions: (a) 75%−95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (100 mmol, pH=6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (b) 95% acetonitrile, 20–200 mmol aqueous ammonium acetate (pH=6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (c) 95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (100 mmol, pH=3.2–7.0), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (d) 95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (100 mmol, pH=6.8), column temperature is 10–40 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1.

Separation of different polar compounds with Sil-Glc-CDs column. (a) five amino acids: (1) cysteine, (2) phenylalanine, (3) isoleucine, (4) proline, (5) alanine acid, mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile, 15% aqueous ammonium acetate solution (100 mmol, pH=6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (b) six saccharides: (1) deoxyribose, (2) ribose, (3) xylose, (4) fructose, (5) glucose, (6) sucrose, mobile phase: 94% acetonitrile, 6% aqueous ammonium acetate solution (100 mmol, pH=6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (c) six ginsenosides: (1) Rh2, (2) Rh1, (3) Rg3, (4) Rg1, (5) Re, (6) Rc, chromatographic conditions are the same as (a); (d) six antibiotics: (1) medimycin, (2) enrofloxacin, (3) roxithromycin, (4) ofloxacin, (5) norfloxacin, (6) azithromycin, chromatographic conditions are the same as (a).

(a) Separation of nine nucleosides and nucleobases with Inspire 5 μm HILIC, Sil-Glc and Sil-Glc-CDs columns, (b) Plot of electrostatic character vs. hydrophilicity character for the evaluated HILIC phases: (1) Sil-Glc-CDs, (2) Sil-Glc, (3) Xbridge HILIC 5 µm, (4) GS-120–5-Aps, (5) Xbridge BEH Amide 5 µm, (6) ZORBAX SB-C18, (7) PRONTOSIL 120–5 Diol; Chromatographic conditions are the same as Fig. 4(b).

(a) Hydrolytic stability of Sil-Glc-CDs columns, mobile phase: 90% acetonitrile and 10% ammonium acetate solution (100 mmol, pH=6.8), column temperature 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1, UV detector: 254 nm. (b) Separation of adenine (1), nicotinic acid (2) and BTMA (3) using three batches of Sil-Glc-CDs columns. Chromatographic conditions are the same as (a).
(a) Standard curves of fructose and glucose; (b) Chromatogram of the separation of fructose and glucose standards and actual samples, chromatographic conditions are the same as Fig. 4(b).
The results of elemental analysis of bare silica, Glc-CDs and the modified silica materials.

head.ce:title: Glucose-based carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bLaboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cUniversity of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new stationary phase based on glucose-derived carbon dots-modified on silica (Sil-Glc-CDs) was prepared and applied in hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). The Sil-Glc-CDs column showed better retention ability and separation selectivity toward polar analytes, including amino acids, saccharides, ginsenosides, antibiotics, nucleosides, and nucleobases when compared with a commercial HILIC column and a home-made glucose-modified silica (Sil-Glc) column. The effect of the organic phase ratio, salt concentration, pH and column temperature on chromatographic retention behavior was investigated. In addition, the column exhibited good stability and column-to-column reproducibility (RSDs 0.80–1.97%, n = 3). Particularly, this column was successfully applied for the determination of the concentration of glucose (2.2 mg mL−1) and fructose (3.4 mg mL−1) in the goji berry solution.

ce:section.ce:para: A variety of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) separation modes has been developed due to the separation requirements of different analytes. The C18 column, which has been commercialized in reversed-phase chromatography mode (NPLC), can satisfy the separation of most compounds, except the polar and hydrophilic compounds [1,2]. This is attributed to the weak interaction between the analyte and the stationary phase, and the analyte is eluted without retention in the stationary phase [3]. Reversed-phase columns functionalized by polar groups have been proposed, or using a mixed-mode stationary phase can solve this problem [4,5]. Another method is to use the hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) mode that firstly reported by Alpert in 1990 [6]. Like the normal phase liquid chromatography (NPLC), HILIC uses a polar substance as the stationary phase and a water-organic solvent as the mobile phase. It has become a benchmark method for separation of hydrophilic compounds [7].

Chromatographic stationary phases such as amino silica gel and bare silica gel can be directly used in HILIC mode, but sometimes their separation performance is not ideal. Therefore, the silica surface can be functionalized by some small molecules [8], polymer [9], or nanomaterials [10] to improve the HILIC performance. Notably, carbon nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes [11], nanodiamonds [12], graphenes [13,14] and carbon nanoparticles [15] have been widely used in sample pretreatment and chromatographic stationary phase and other separation science field because of great specific surface area, excellent mechanical performances and high adsorption capacity.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel member of the carbon nanomaterial family, have unique fluorescent properties, chemical stability, and biocompatibility [16,17]. It is widely used in bioimaging [18], biosensing [19], photocatalysis [20] etc. Besides, CDs are also widely used in sample preparation owing to the surface of the CDs contains a great number of functional groups, which can improve the hydrophilicity and dispersibility of the material [21–23]. CDs are small in size, water-soluble, and easy to be functionalized, implying that CDs can be employed as chromatographic separation materials in HPLC and GC [24,25].

Our group previously reported the work of preparation of CDs-modified silica as a new type of chromatographic stationary phases [26–29]. However, the results showed that the chromatographic properties of stationary phases prepared from different CDs were also changed. That is to say, the features of CDs, to a certain extent, determine the chromatographic performance of CDs-modified silica. Glucose has the advantages of low price and readily available and can be used for preparing water-soluble CDs. In this work, CDs were developed by the hydrothermal synthesis method with glucose as the carbon source and then bonded to the surface of spherical silica gel to obtain a glucose-derived carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase (Sil-Glc-CDs). The chromatographic performance of this new column was carefully examined and showed a wide separation range including nucleosides/base, amino acid, ginsenosides and saccharides. Besides, our prepared column can also be used for quantitative detection of the content of monosaccharide in the Goji berry solution.

Spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore diameter: 90 Å, surface area: 306 m2 g−1) was bought from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd. Glucose, phosphoric acid potassium dehydrogenate was purchased by Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). 3-Isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (IPTS, 95%) and triethylenediamine (DABCO) were available from Aladdin Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), chromatographic pure acetonitrile (ACN), and ammonium acetate(≥99.0%) are all obtained from Aladdin Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Toluene and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were bought from Cologne Chemical (Sichuan, China). Ethanol (99.7%) from Shanxi Tongjie Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff of 500) was purchased from Beijing Suo Laibao Technology (Beijing, China), amino acids, nucleosides and nucleobases, antibiotics, saccharides and other test standards were bought from Aladdin Biotechnology (Shanghai, China).

Inspire 5 μm HILIC columns are bought from Dikma (150 × 4.6 mm i.d.) for reference. Agilent 1260 High-Performance Liquid Chromatography - Diode Array Detector (HPLC-DAD, Agilent, USA), HPLC equipment using Shimadzu the LC-10AT system, including the high-pressure pump, SPD-10AV UV/vis detector and Alltech 3300 Evaporative Light Scattering Detector with GCK3302 Air Generator (China BCHP Analytical Technology Institute). IFS 120HR Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker, Germany) was used to measure infrared spectral data. Elemental analysis results were provided by the Vario EL III elemental analyzer (EA, Elementar, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy image results were obtained from the Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope images were collected from the FV1200 laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Olympus, Japan). LGJ-10 Freeze Dryer (Beijing Songyuan Huaxing Technology Development Co., Ltd.) was used to dry carbon dots. Ultrapure water came from Milli-Q ultrapure water purification equipment (Millipore, Billerica, United States).

Glc-CDs were prepared according to a modified method, as reported in the reference [30]. As shown in Fig. 1(a), glucose, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate with a mass ratio of 1: 26 are accurately weighed. The mixture was sonicated after adding a specific volume of water, and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and was bubbled to remove oxygen from the solution with N2. The reaction was heated in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h, and then the black impurities were removed by centrifugation and filtration. After dialysis for 10 h, the dialysis solution was freeze-dried, and the product was re-dispersed in ethanol for 2 h. The potassium dihydrogen phosphate is precipitated because the insolubility in ethanol and the supernatant was dried by rotary evaporation until a small amount of liquid remained, then Glc-CDs was obtained through freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Sil-Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc stationary phases
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1b and 1c, firstly, 3 g of silica was dispersed in 20 mL of anhydrous toluene, 6 mmol of IPTS was added. Subsequently, the dispersion was heated to 120 °C and mechanically stirred for 24 h under N2 atmosphere. Then the product was washed with anhydrous toluene and anhydrous DMF, respectively. Secondly, IPTS-modified silica was redispersed in anhydrous DMF, specific amounts of Glc-CDs was added, and 0.3 mmol of DABCO was added as a catalyst. After reacted under N2 atmosphere for 24 h, the modified silica was washed with anhydrous DMF, water, and ethanol repeatedly, and then dried at 60 °C. The preparation of the Sil-Glc stationary phase was similar to Sil-Glc-CDs, except the 250 mg Glc-CDs was replaced by 250 mg glucose.

Sil-Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc were packed into the stainless steel column (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) through the slurry method. In a word, 2.2 g modified silica was ultrasonically dispersed in 22 mL methanol, and the resulting mixture was driven into a stainless steel column under a pressure of 40 MPa using methanol as displacing solvent.

Goji berry is a traditional herbal medicine in China, with high health care and medicinal value because it is rich in polysaccharides, carotenoids, flavonoids, amino acids, and vitamins [31]. Among them, the polysaccharide is the main biologically active compound and is composed of monosaccharides such as fructose, glucose, etc. [32,33] It is believed that polysaccharides played a significant role in the prevention and remedy of cancer [34], diabetes [35] and immunodeficiency [36–38]. Therefore, it has significant meaning for the separation and analysis of polysaccharides from the goji berry.

The extraction of polysaccharide from goji berry is reported in the literature [39]. As described below: 2.2 g goji berry was added into 75 mL ultrapure water, preheated to 60 °C in a water bath for 1 h, and transferred to ultrasonic extraction at room temperature for 30 min. The supernatant and TFA (2 mol L − 1) with the volume ratio of 5:1 was directly reacted without stirring in an oven at 120 °C for 2 h. It was filtered with a 0.45 μm filter and then cooled to room temperature; 2 mL supernatant was put in a water bath at 90 °C to remove TFA. Finally, the sample was re-dissolved with ultra-pure water and filtered before injection.

The TEM images are shown in Fig. 2 a-c, including bare silica gel, Glc-CDs, and Sil-Glc-CDs. The size of Glc-CDs can be observed at about 4.5 nm. And the surface of Sil-Glc-CDs was uniformly coated with Glc-CDs, which indicates that Glc-CDs were successfully bonded to the surface of the silica gel. LSCM images of Sil-Glc-CDs (Fig. 2d) revealed green fluorescence on the surface of Sil-Glc-CDs in the bright-field. This phenomenon is not seen in Sil-Glc stationary phase (Fig. S1). It again proves that Glc-CDs were grafted on the silica surface successfully.

Fig. S2 represents FT-IR spectroscopy images of Glucose, Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc-CDs. It was confirmed that glucose is carbonized to Glc-CDs under hydrothermal conditions. Compared with the infrared spectrum of glucose, hydrophilic functional groups are generated on the surface of Glc-CDs, such as strong at 1603 cm−1 and 3437 cm−1. The absorption peak indicates the presence of CO and -OH. Besides, 1263 cm−1 and 1069 cm−1 are symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibration peaks of COC, respectively, which is the result that Glc-CDs is partially oxidized by hydrothermal reaction. In the infrared spectrum of Sil-Glc-CDs, 1654 cm−1 represents the characteristic peak of CO in the carbamate, 1550 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of secondary amine. The strong absorption of 1095 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of Si-O on silica gel or silane reagent.

Elemental analysis results of silica gel, Sil-NCO, Sil-Glc-CDs, and Sil-Glc can be found in Table 1 . The elemental content of Sil-Glc-CDs (C 7.08% and H 1.04%) was increased significantly compared with that of Sil-NCO (C 4.87% and H 0.90%), which proved again that the synthesis of the stationary phase is successful. The elemental analysis results of the Sil-Glc stationary phase (C 6.97% and H 0.85%) also can be found in Table 1.

In HILIC mode, the types of stationary phases and elution conditions are essential factors affecting the separation and selectivity of polar compounds. Therefore, it is vital to select the optimal chromatographic separation conditions for the separation performance and retention mechanism of hydrophilic chromatography. For the Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase, nucleosides and nucleobases were chosen as the analytes, the effects of different chromatographic conditions on the separation were explored by adjusting the acetonitrile content, buffer concentration, mobile phase pH and column temperature.

The effect of the content of the organic solvents in the mobile phase (from 75% to 95%) on retention time was investigated, as shown in Fig. 3(a). With the acetonitrile content increasing, the retention time was also increased. It is indicated that the Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase conforms to the classical HILIC mode. Buffer concentration is another factor affecting the retention time of the analytes. Fig. 3(b) denotes the relationship between different buffer concentrations (20–200 mmol) and retention time. With the increase of ammonium acetate concentration, the retention time of most analytes is enhanced, probably because of the thickening of its hydrophilic layer, the hydrophilic partitioning plays a dominant role in the retention of analytes, but the adenine is more likely affected by electrostatic interactions. The reason why the pH of the mobile phase influences its retention is that it can change the charge state between the stationary phase and analyte. In this experiment, the pH value was chosen in the range of 3.2–7.0 to investigate the influence on retention time. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c), in the acidic environment, due to the protonation of the silanol group on the surface of the analyte and the stationary phase, the electrostatic repulsion force is enhanced and the retention time is weakened. The column temperature also has a positive influence on its retention. The Van't Hoff Eq. (1) is commonly used to explore the relationship between the retention factor and the column temperature [40]. The effect of the temperature variation range of 10–40 °C on the retention was also checked as shown in Fig. 3(d). The ln k and 1/T of all analytes are linear. The linear correlation coefficient and the Van't Hoff equation parameters are shown in Table S1. Except for cytosine, guanosine nucleoside and astragalus nucleoside, the correlation coefficients of other compounds are all above 0.99. The ΔHо of all analytes was higher than TΔSо, indicating that the morphological effect of solute was more pronounced in the transfer of a solute from the mobile phase to the stationary phase. The retention time of the analyte was getting shorter when the column temperature became higher, and this implies that the effect of column temperature on retention is mainly dominated by hydrophilic partitioning, the difference in slope of different analytes may be due to the size of the adsorption interaction [41].
(1)
ln k =
Δ
H ∘
R T
+
Δ
S ∘
R
+ ln
Φ

The hydrophilic properties of the Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase were tested by selecting nucleosides and nucleobases, ginsenosides, amino acids, antibiotics, and saccharides, as shown in Figs. 4 and 5
(a). The structures of these compounds are shown in Fig. S3-S6. It is clear to see that the Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase has a better separation in the nine nucleosides and nucleobases than the commercial column and Sil-Glc column, as shown in Fig. 5(a). Five amino acids include cysteine, phenylalanine, isoleucine, valine, and alanine, can be separated within 10 min with the Sil-Glc-CDs column as shown in Fig. 4(a). Sil-Glc-CDs have a good separation effect relative to six kinds of saccharides (Fig. 4b). The ginsenoside mixture of six triterpenoid compounds including Rh2, Rh1, Rg3, Rg1, Re, and Rc, can be baseline separated as shown in Fig. 4(c). And six antibiotics including medimycin, enrofloxacin, roxithromycin, ofloxacin, norfloxacin, and azithromycin, can be completely separated as shown in Fig. 4(d), but the tailing phenomenon is a little serious, probably due to the silanol group on the silica gel and the alkaline antibiotic analytes have a strong interaction, resulting in a peak tail. The chromatographic parameters including symmetry factor, plate number, and resolution, can be found in Table S2-S4 (Supporting information). Take the plate number of guanosines on Sil-Glc-CDs as an example, and the column efficiency is 43,800 plates/m.

Besides, since the glucose molecule is the raw material for the Sil-Glc-CDs, Sil-Glc column and an Inspire 5 μm HILIC column were selected as the reference columns to separate different compounds. The results are shown in Fig. 5(a), and Fig. S7–10, that the retention of the nine nucleosides and nucleobases on the Inspire 5 μm HILIC column was similar to the Sil-Glc-CDs, but the Inspire 5 μm HILIC column did not separate well for both cytidine and cytosine. The separation of nine nucleosides and nucleobases is weak in the Sil-Glc column, probably because the interaction sites of glucose molecules with silica are less than the sites of action of glucose carbon dots and silica. Besides, the separation results of other polar compounds also again confirmed the behavior of Sil-Glc-CDs has better separation selectivity than commonly used commercial silica and Sil-Glc columns.

Cytosine, uracil, and benzyl trimethylammonium chloride (BTMA) were selected as test probes for the comparison of our new column and the commercial columns [42]. Cytosine/uracil was used as the x-axis to test the hydrophilic property of columns, BTMA/cytosine was used as the y-axis to measure the electrostatic characteristics. The result was shown in Fig. 5(b). The number “1″ means the Sil-Glc-CDs column. The value of cytosine/uracil is significantly higher than most of the referenced columns, indicating the hydrophilic property of Sil-Glc-CDs is good, and it also has a specific cation-exchange capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of the Sil-Glc-CDs column
ce:section.ce:para: To test the stability of the column during long-term use, positively charged BTMA, neutral adenine, and negatively charged nicotinic acid were selected as test mixture. Under the same chromatographic conditions, the column was rinsed continuously for 30 h and injected the mixture every one hour [43]. The ratio of retention time at any given hour (tR,n) and injection at the first time (tR,0) as y-axis, the washing time is the x-axis to obtain a chromatogram, see Fig. 6(a) and the characteristics of each commercial column were shown in Table S5. None of the three scatter plots rise or fall significantly, implying the silica surface of Si–O–Si bonds are not broken, exhibits excellent hydrolytic stability of column. Adenine, nicotinic acid and BTMA were also used to test batch-to-batch reproducibility (Fig. 6b). As listed in Table S6, The RSDs value ranges from 0.80 to 1.97% (n = 3). Besides, we also characterized three batches of Sil-Glc-CDs by element analysis (Table S7). And the results showed that the elemental analysis results were the same, indicating that the column could be prepared repeatedly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of fructose and glucose from goji berry solution
ce:section.ce:para:
Accurately configure the standard solution of fructose and glucose in the concentration range of 0.5~3.5 mg mL−1, the standard curve was obtained, as shown in Fig. 6(a). The linear regression equations of fructose and glucose are shown in (2) and (3), and the correlation coefficients (R2) are 0.9995 and 0.9977, respectively. The Sil-Glc-CDs stationary phase was used to separate and quantify the fructose and glucose from the goji berry solution (see Fig. 6b). It was found that the fructose concentration in the sputum sample was 3.4 mg mL−1, and the glucose concentration was 2 mg mL−1. Another two commercially available columns including GS-120-5-APS and Xbridge HILIC 5 µm were also used to determine fructose and glucose in goji berry. The result is shown in Fig. S11, fructose and glucose in goji berry also can be separated on these two columns. But amino column needed more extended time to determine fructose and glucose Fig. 7 .
(2)
y F
= 1686.4 x − 521.55
(3)
y G
= 1285.3 x − 357.82

A chromatographic stationary phase based on glucose-derived CDs-bonded silica gel was successfully prepared. In the HILIC mode, the column was able to separate multiple polar compounds and displayed higher separation selectivity than Inspire 5 μm HILIC column and glucose-modified silica columns. By examining the chromatographic conditions, it was found that the separation performance of the chromatographic stationary phase was affected by the partitioning mechanism and the adsorption mechanism. Moreover, the stationary phase was also successfully used for the quantification of glucose and fructose in an aqueous solution of goji berry. This new stationary phase is hopefully to be used for the separation and analysis of more complex samples.

Ning Yuan: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Jia Chen: Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Tianpei Cai: Methodology, Software. Zhan Li: Writing - review & editing. Ming Guan: Writing - review & editing. Liang Zhao: Writing - review & editing. Hongdeng Qiu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21822407, and 21675164) and Xinjiang “Tianshan Youth Plan” Project (No. 2017Q027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2020.460930.

J. Chromatogr. A

Chin. Chem. Lett.
